interactions.summary: Jiang presents Jack Ruby's killing of Oswald, the police reaction, RFK's later assassination, and Sirhan Sirhan's disputed guilt as additional facts that in his view do not add up.

He lists suspicious 9/11 details: debris shipped to China before investigation, the unprecedented pancake-theory collapse, three buildings from two planes, intact passports and boarding passes, Operation Northwoods, and a Lone Gunman episode with a government-controlled plane plot.

Jiang turns Larry Silverstein into a darkly comic case of stupidity becoming luck: buying an asbestos-burdened lease, overinsuring it, receiving billions after 9/11, and missing work because of a dermatology appointment.

He pivots sarcastically to reasons one should reject conspiracy theories and believe the government: governments never lie, they are too incompetent, and if they could conspire they could also cover everything up.

claims.claim: Oswald's public killing by Jack Ruby is framed as suspicious because Ruby got close, Oswald was held in place, and a trained officer froze instead of reacting.
Jiang argues that 9/11 evidence handling was suspicious because debris was shipped to China quickly instead of being preserved for investigation.
The official passport and boarding-pass evidence is presented sarcastically as implausibly lucky proof because bodies and luggage supposedly disappeared while identity documents survived.
Operation Northwoods and The Lone Gunman are used as pattern evidence that government-staged false-flag plane scenarios were imaginable before 9/11.
Silverstein's lease, insurance valuation, payout, and absence from work are used as a luck/profit chain that Jiang treats as suspicious around 9/11.

signature_moments.moment: Silverstein becomes a moral joke: do not be selfish, just be lucky.
signature_moments.source_phrase: The luckiest man in the world
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The joke condenses Jiang's suspicion into a memorable rule about power, profit, and improbable timing.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang performs belief in the official story as deadpan absurdity: the best reason is that government never lies.
signature_moments.source_phrase: The first reason, of course, is the government will never lie to us.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This marks the transition from suspicious details to a theory of why evidence is allowed to remain visible.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: A 1962 plan Jiang describes as hijacking civilian aircraft and blaming Cuba to justify war.
The explanation Jiang gives for the official account of the towers collapsing floor by floor after steel frames weakened.

chronology_notes.note: Jiang dates Operation Northwoods to 1962 and The Lone Gunman episode to six months before 9/11.

uncertainty_notes.note: The transcript says '$3.5 million' in one payout sentence, but context indicates Jiang is discussing billion-dollar insurance values; avoid over-indexing the exact amount without checking source audio.
